lessons.title: Boundary Law Principles Review
learning_objectives: Recall the senior-rights / order-of-conveyance rule and apply it to a split parcel.
Rank the priority of calls (controlling elements) used to retrace a boundary.
Distinguish original-survey retracement from a new survey and the 'footsteps' doctrine.
Apply proportionate measurement (single and double) to distribute excess and deficiency.
key_takeaways: Boundary retracement follows the original surveyor's footsteps; the goal is the location the parties accepted, not a mathematically 'perfect' line.
Priority of calls (Texas/Stafford v. King): natural monuments > artificial monuments > calls for adjoinder > course (bearing) > distance > area/quantity, all subordinate to the writing's intent.
Senior rights govern conflicts between adjoining deeds carved from a common grantor: the first conveyance gets its full record; the junior takes the remainder.
Excess or deficiency between found original corners is distributed by proportionate (relative) measurement, not dumped into one lot.

content.html:
What boundary law tests on the SIT

The boundary-law portion of the SIT/FS exam rewards principles and priorities, not memorized statutes. Almost every question reduces to one idea: a retracing surveyor's job is to find where the boundary already is—the location the original parties created and accepted—not to compute a new 'better' line. You are gathering and weighing evidence. When the written words of a deed conflict with the marks on the ground, a hierarchy tells you which controls. Expect short scenario questions ('a fence has stood 40 years', 'the deed calls for an iron pin but none is found', 'two deeds overlap') where the right answer is the principle that resolves the conflict. This lesson is a high-yield refresher; the underlying doctrines were developed in Modules 13–15.

Priority of calls

When the elements of a description disagree, retracement follows a priority of calls. The classic Texas ordering from Stafford v. King (1867) is: (1) natural monuments (rivers, rock outcrops, established trees), (2) artificial monuments (iron pins, set stones, fences relied on), (3) calls for adjoinder (the line of an adjoining senior survey), (4) course / bearing, (5) distance, then (6) area or quantity. Higher elements control lower ones because they are harder to fabricate and better reflect what the parties saw and intended. But the hierarchy is a presumption, not a straitjacket: it yields to the clearly expressed intent of the grant and to evidence that a lower element is more reliable in the specific case.

  • Monuments over measurements is the single most-tested idea.
  • Distance is weak—it is the easiest element to mis-measure and is routinely overridden by a found monument.
  • Area/quantity is the weakest and almost never controls.

content.text: Texas differs: many state exams cite the general common-law order, but Texas grounds its priority of calls in Stafford v. King (1867). Same idea—natural monuments down to quantity—but cite the Texas case if the question is Texas-specific.

rows: First deed out of a common grantor gets its full record dimensions; junior takes the remainder.
Two adjoining deeds overlap or leave a gap
Find and honor the original surveyor's lines and corners, even if erroneous.
Found original monument disagrees with record distance
Natural mon. > artificial mon. > adjoinder > bearing > distance > area.
Distribute excess/deficiency in proportion to record between found original corners.
Block of lots measures long or short
Adverse possession / acquiescence / estoppel can move a line off the record.

content.html:
Proportionate measurement

When the total distance between two found original corners differs from the record (excess or deficiency), and no intervening original monuments survive, the difference is distributed by proportionate (relative) measurement. Each interior interval keeps its record proportion of the measured total. Single proportion distributes along one line; double proportion (used for lost PLSS section corners) balances between the four controlling corners north–south and east–west. The key exam distinction: a corner is lost (re-set by proportion) only when its position cannot be recovered from any acceptable evidence. If it can be recovered—by an existing monument, by accessories, or by reliable testimony—it is merely obliterated, and you recover it from the evidence rather than proportioning. Never proportion away a found original monument.

content.text: Lost vs. obliterated is a favorite trap: 'obliterated' corners are RECOVERED from evidence; only 'lost' corners are RESTORED by proportionate measurement.

content.alt: Block of lots showing record vs measured distance and proportional distribution of excess
content.caption: Figure: Single proportionate measurement distributing excess across a block of equal record lots.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Retracement honors the original footsteps; you locate the existing boundary, not a new one.
Priority of calls: natural > artificial monuments > adjoinder > bearing > distance > area, subject to intent.
Senior rights: earliest conveyance from a common grantor controls; junior takes the remainder.
Distribute excess/deficiency by proportion; recover obliterated corners from evidence but restore only lost corners by proportioning.

flashcards.definition: The hierarchy used when deed elements conflict: natural monuments > artificial monuments > calls for adjoinder > course (bearing) > distance > area/quantity, all subordinate to the grant's intent.
When two parcels are carved from a common grantor, the parcel conveyed first (senior) is entitled to its full record dimensions; the later (junior) parcel takes whatever remains.

An obliterated corner has no remaining physical mark but can be recovered from acceptable evidence (accessories, testimony); a lost corner cannot be recovered from any evidence and must be restored by proportionate measurement.

lessons.title: Legal Descriptions Review
learning_objectives: Identify the major types of legal description (metes-and-bounds, lot-and-block, strip/centerline, PLSS aliquot) and when each is used.
Read a metes-and-bounds call set and recognize a proper point of beginning, closure, and monument calls.
Spot defects: ambiguous calls, missing closure, conflicting bearings vs. monuments.
Apply the rules of construction that courts use to interpret an ambiguous description.
key_takeaways: A legal description must identify a unique parcel; metes-and-bounds (the Texas norm) traces the perimeter by bearing-distance calls from a defined POB back to closure.
Lot-and-block descriptions incorporate a recorded plat by reference; the plat supplies the dimensions and monuments.
Specific (monument) calls control over general (bearing/distance) calls when they conflict—the writing is read to carry out the parties' intent.
A description that fails to close, omits a POB, or is patently ambiguous on its face may be void; latent ambiguities are cured with extrinsic evidence.

content.html:
Why descriptions matter on the exam

A legal description is the written half of a boundary; the monuments are the physical half. The exam tests whether you can read a description, locate the parcel, and detect defects. In Texas, the dominant form is metes-and-bounds because most land traces to Spanish/Mexican grants and Republic/State patents rather than a federal PLSS grid. Even modern subdivisions ultimately rest on a metes-and-bounds boundary of the parent tract. Know the anatomy of a call set and the rules courts use when the words are unclear.

Anatomy of a metes-and-bounds description

A complete metes-and-bounds description contains: a caption (county, survey/abstract, parent tract), a clearly fixed Point of Beginning (POB) tied to an identifiable monument or coordinate, a series of calls (each a bearing and distance, often with a monument call: 'N 45°00' E, 150.00 ft to a 1/2-inch iron rod set'), and a closing call returning to the POB ('thence ... to the Point of Beginning'). The perimeter should close mathematically. A good description also recites area and basis of bearings. Watch for:

  • Calls that recite both a monument and a bearing/distance—if they disagree, the monument controls.
  • A 'more or less' on the area—area is the weakest element and yields to the perimeter.
  • Missing or floating POB—fatal unless cured by reference to a plat or adjoiner.

rows: Perimeter traced by bearing/distance and monument calls from a POB.
Texas norm; original tracts, splits, easements
Reference to a recorded subdivision plat (Lot 7, Block C, of ...).
A baseline or centerline with offsets/widths (roads, pipelines).
Quarter/quarter-quarter of a section, township, range.

content.html:
Rules of construction for ambiguous descriptions

When a description is unclear, courts and surveyors apply settled rules of construction to find the parties' intent: (1) the instrument is read as a whole; (2) specific calls control general calls (a monument call governs a mere bearing/distance); (3) where words conflict with figures, the controlling element is the one most consistent with intent; (4) a description is construed against the grantor who wrote it; and (5) patent ambiguities (apparent on the face, e.g., a call to a nonexistent monument with no other clue) may void the deed, while latent ambiguities (clear on the page but failing on the ground) are resolved with extrinsic evidence. The surveyor's task is to harmonize—reject an element only when it cannot be reconciled.

content.text: Exam tip: 'specific controls general' and 'monument controls bearing/distance' are the same idea wearing two hats. If a call names a found monument, that location wins.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Metes-and-bounds is the Texas standard; know POB, calls, closing call, basis of bearings, and area.
Specific/monument calls control general bearing-distance calls; the deed is read as a whole for intent.
Area ('more or less') is the weakest element and yields to the perimeter.
Patent ambiguity can void a description; latent ambiguity is cured with extrinsic evidence.

flashcards.definition: The fixed starting point of a metes-and-bounds description, tied to an identifiable monument or coordinate, to which the closing call returns.
flashcards.hint_1: Where the perimeter starts and ends.

flashcards.definition: Rule of construction: a specific call (e.g., a named monument) controls a general call (e.g., a bare bearing/distance) when the two conflict.
A patent ambiguity is apparent on the face of the description and may void it; a latent ambiguity appears only when applying the words to the ground and is cured with extrinsic evidence.

lessons.title: Texas Land Records Review
learning_objectives: Trace a Texas chain of title from sovereign grant/patent through the county deed records.
Describe the role of the General Land Office (GLO) versus the county clerk's records.
Explain the Texas recording act (notice statute) and how recording protects a bona fide purchaser.
Locate original survey field notes, abstracts, and patents needed for retracement research.
key_takeaways: Texas titles originate from a sovereign grant (Spanish/Mexican) or a State patent, archived at the GLO, not from a federal PLSS.
The GLO holds original field notes, sketches, and patents for original land grants; the county clerk records subsequent conveyances (deeds).
Texas is a 'notice' recording state: an unrecorded conveyance is void as to a later bona fide purchaser for value without notice.
Good retracement research follows the chain backward from the current deed to the original grant, pulling field notes and adjoiner deeds.

content.html:
Where Texas land titles come from

Unlike public-land (PLSS) states, Texas kept its public domain when it joined the Union, so Texas titles trace to a sovereign grant—Spanish, Mexican, Republic of Texas, or State of Texas—rather than to a federal township-and-range survey. The original act that severed land from the sovereign is the patent (or grant). The General Land Office (GLO) is the archive: it holds the original field notes, sketches, and patents, organized by survey and abstract number within each county. For retracement, the GLO record is often your best evidence of the original surveyor's footsteps.

GLO vs. county records

Two record systems matter. The GLO governs the origin of title—original grants, patents, and the field notes that define the original survey boundaries. The county clerk governs subsequent conveyances—the deeds, plats, easements, and liens recorded in the Official Public Records (Deed Records). To research a parcel you typically pull the current deed at the county, run the chain backward grantor-to-grantee, identify the original survey and abstract, then go to the GLO for the original field notes. A complete chain of title runs from the sovereign grant all the way to the present owner with no gaps.

content.text: Texas differs: there is no federal PLSS for original Texas land. References to 'sections, townships, and ranges' generally do not apply; Texas uses surveys, leagues/labors, and abstract numbers archived at the GLO.

content.html:
The recording act and bona fide purchasers

Texas is a notice recording state (Property Code §13.001). An unrecorded conveyance or lien is void as to a subsequent bona fide purchaser (BFP) for valuable consideration who takes without notice of the prior interest. Recording an instrument gives constructive notice to the world, protecting the grantee's priority. 'Notice' includes actual, constructive (record), and inquiry notice—visible possession or a fence can be enough to put a buyer on inquiry. For the surveyor, the lesson is practical: the record is not always complete, and what is on the ground (occupation, monuments) can carry legal weight even when it is not in the deed records.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Texas titles begin with a sovereign grant/patent archived at the GLO, organized by survey and abstract number.
The GLO holds original field notes and patents; the county clerk records later deeds, plats, and liens.
Texas is a notice state: recording gives constructive notice and protects a BFP without notice (Prop. Code §13.001).
Retracement research runs the chain backward from current deed to the original grant, gathering field notes and adjoiner deeds.

flashcards.definition: The instrument by which the sovereign (Spain, Mexico, Republic, or State of Texas) first conveyed public land into private ownership; the root of a Texas chain of title.
flashcards.hint_1: The grant that severs land from the sovereign.

flashcards.definition: The Texas state agency that archives original land-grant records—patents, field notes, and sketches—organized by survey and abstract number.
flashcards.hint_1: Keeper of original Texas land records.

flashcards.definition: Texas's rule (Prop. Code §13.001) that an unrecorded conveyance is void as to a subsequent bona fide purchaser for value without notice; recording provides constructive notice.

lessons.title: Construction Surveying Review
learning_objectives: Set up and stake horizontal and vertical curves from PI, R, and design elements.
Compute curve geometry: T, L, M, E, and chord from R and I.
Compute elevations on a vertical curve and locate the high/low point.
Apply slope-staking and grade/offset stakes for construction layout.
key_takeaways: Horizontal circular curve: T = R·tan(I/2), L = R·I(rad) = R·toRad(I), LC = 2R·sin(I/2); the PC is at PI − T along the back tangent.
Stationing is continuous along the alignment; PC = PI − T and PT = PC + L (NOT PI + T).
Equal-tangent vertical curve elevation: y = g1·x + (A/(2L))·x², where A = g2 − g1 (percent) and L is curve length in stations.
Construction stakes carry a cut/fill to design grade; slope stakes mark where the design slope meets existing ground.

content.html:
Construction layout on the exam

Construction questions are computational and formula-driven—you stake what the engineer designed. The two big topics are horizontal curves (circular arcs joining two tangents) and vertical curves (parabolas joining two grades). You should be able to start from the given design elements (PI station, radius, intersection angle, grades, curve length) and produce the layout values: tangent length, curve length, key-point stations, and elevations. Memorize the small set of curve formulas—they appear on nearly every FS/SIT exam.

content.latex: T = R\tan\!\frac{I}{2}, \quad L = R\,I_{rad} = R\cdot\frac{\pi I}{180}, \quad LC = 2R\sin\!\frac{I}{2}
content.label: Horizontal circular curve — tangent, arc length, long chord

content.html:
Horizontal curve geometry

A simple circular curve is defined by its radius R and intersection (deflection) angle I. From those: the tangent T = R·tan(I/2) is the distance from the PI back to the PC (and forward to the PT); the arc length L = R·I in radians; the long chord LC = 2R·sin(I/2). Two more layout values: the middle ordinate M = R(1 − cos(I/2)) and the external distance E = R(sec(I/2) − 1). Stationing is continuous along the alignment, so you must walk the curve, not the tangents: Sta PC = Sta PI − T and Sta PT = Sta PC + L. The most common error is computing PT as PI + T—wrong, because the alignment runs along the arc, which is shorter than going out to the PI and back.

content.text: Common trap: PT = PC + L (arc), NOT PI + T. Walking out to the PI and back overstates the alignment length by 2T − L.

content.latex: y = g_1 x + \frac{(g_2 - g_1)}{2L}x^2, \qquad x_{turn} = \frac{-g_1 L}{g_2 - g_1}
content.label: Equal-tangent vertical curve — elevation and turning point

content.html:
Vertical curves

An equal-tangent vertical curve is a parabola joining an incoming grade g1 to an outgoing grade g2 over length L. Let the algebraic grade difference be A = g2 − g1 (in percent). The elevation at a distance x (in stations) past the BVC is Elev = ElevBVC + g1·x + (A/2L)·x², with grades expressed as percent and x, L in stations of 100 ft. The high or low point occurs where the slope is zero, at x = −g1·L / A (stations from BVC); it exists on the curve only if g1 and g2 have opposite signs. A crest curve (g1 > 0, g2 < 0) has a high point; a sag curve has a low point—critical for drainage and sight distance.

Grade, offset, and slope stakes

Layout in the field is delivered through stakes. A grade stake (or hub-and-tack with a guard) carries a marked cut or fill to design grade at that station. Offset stakes are set a known horizontal distance off the centerline (so they survive excavation) and reference the centerline grade plus the offset. Slope stakes mark the catch point—where the designed cut or fill slope (e.g., 2:1) intersects existing ground—and are set by trial: estimate the offset, measure ground, recompute, repeat until the slope closes on the ground. Always label whether the figure is a cut (remove material) or fill (add material) and to which surface (subgrade vs. finished).

content.alt: Horizontal circular curve showing PI, PC, PT, tangent T, arc L, long chord, and intersection angle I
content.caption: Figure: Simple circular curve elements—PI, PC, PT, T, L, LC, and intersection angle I.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: T = R·tan(I/2), L = R·toRad(I), LC = 2R·sin(I/2); also M = R(1−cos(I/2)), E = R(sec(I/2)−1).
Station the alignment along the arc: PC = PI − T, PT = PC + L.
Vertical curve: Elev = Elev_BVC + g1·x + (A/2L)·x², high/low at x = −g1·L/A.
Field stakes carry cut/fill; slope stakes locate the catch point of the design slope on existing ground.

flashcards.definition: For a simple circular curve, T = R·tan(I/2): the distance from the PI to the PC (and from the PI to the PT), measured along the tangents.
Stationing runs continuously along the alignment, so PT = PC + L (the arc length), never PI + T.
flashcards.hint_1: Walk the curve, not the tangents.

flashcards.definition: A parabolic curve joining two grades over length L; elevation = Elev_BVC + g1·x + (A/2L)·x² with A = g2 − g1, x and L in stations.

lessons.title: Topographic Surveying Review
learning_objectives: Describe topographic data-collection methods (total station, RTK GNSS, LiDAR, photogrammetry) and their accuracy regimes.
Compute photogrammetric Ground Sample Distance (GSD) from sensor and flight parameters.
Interpret contours: rules of contour behavior, interval selection, and DTM/DEM/DSM distinctions.
Plan adequate point density and breaklines for a faithful surface model.
key_takeaways: Topo deliverables are surfaces and contours; choose a method and density that resolve the smallest feature of interest.
GSD ≈ (pixel size × flight height) / focal length; smaller GSD = finer detail. For drones, GSD ≈ (sensor-pixel-pitch × H) / f.
Contours never cross or split; they V upstream at streams and point downhill on ridges; spacing shows slope (close = steep).
A DTM/DEM is bare earth; a DSM includes vegetation and structures—breaklines force the surface to honor grade changes.

content.html:
Topographic surveying scope

A topographic survey captures the shape of the ground and the features on it—elevations, breaklines, drainage, and improvements—and delivers a surface model and contours. The exam tests method selection (total station vs. RTK GNSS vs. LiDAR vs. photogrammetry), contour interpretation, and a few computations, most notably Ground Sample Distance (GSD) for aerial/UAS work. The governing principle is fitness for purpose: pick the data density and accuracy that resolve the smallest feature the design cares about.

rows: Hard detail, high accuracy, obstructed sky

content.label: Ground Sample Distance (p = pixel pitch, H = flight height AGL, f = focal length)

content.html:
Ground Sample Distance (GSD)

For aerial and UAS imagery, the GSD is the ground distance represented by one image pixel—the practical resolution of the survey. It follows directly from similar triangles: GSD = (p × H) / f, where p is the sensor's pixel pitch (physical size of one pixel on the chip), H is the flying height above ground level, and f is the lens focal length (same units as p, e.g., mm). Lower flight = smaller GSD = finer detail, at the cost of more images and flight time. A common planning rule: choose H so the GSD is roughly half the smallest feature you must resolve. Keep units consistent—if p is in mm and f is in mm, H and GSD share the same length unit.

Reading contours

Contours are lines of equal elevation, and they obey rules the exam loves: they never cross or branch (a single contour has one elevation); they close on themselves or run off the sheet; spacing encodes slope—closely spaced contours are steep, widely spaced are flat; they form a 'V' pointing upstream when crossing a drainage and a 'V' or 'U' pointing downhill on a ridge; and concentric closed contours indicate a hill (or, with hachures, a depression). Pick a contour interval matched to relief and scale—too coarse hides grade, too fine clutters. Distinguish the surface models: a DTM/DEM is bare earth; a DSM includes vegetation and buildings. Breaklines (curbs, ditch flowlines, ridges) force the triangulated surface to honor abrupt grade changes.

content.text: Exam tip: contours 'V upstream'—the point of the V aims up the valley toward higher ground. On a ridge the V points downhill. Closely spaced = steep.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Match collection method and density to the smallest feature the design requires.
GSD = (pixel pitch × flight height) / focal length; lower flight gives finer GSD.
Contours never cross; spacing shows slope; they V upstream across drainages.
DTM/DEM = bare earth, DSM = surface with features; breaklines enforce grade breaks.

flashcards.definition: The ground distance covered by one image pixel: GSD = (pixel pitch × flight height) / focal length. It sets the effective resolution of aerial/UAS imagery.
flashcards.hint_1: Fly lower → smaller GSD → more detail.

flashcards.definition: The constant vertical elevation difference between adjacent contour lines; chosen to match terrain relief and map scale.
A Digital Terrain Model (DTM/DEM) represents the bare-earth surface; a Digital Surface Model (DSM) includes vegetation and structures on top of the ground.

lessons.title: Professional Practice Review
learning_objectives: State the surveyor's primary professional duty and standard of care.
Apply core ethical obligations: competence, independence, confidentiality, and honest dealing.
Explain liability theories (negligence, breach of contract) and how standards of practice define the duty.
Identify business-practice essentials: contracts, scope, certifications, and record retention.
key_takeaways: The surveyor's overriding duty is to protect the public; the legal yardstick is the standard of care of a reasonably prudent surveyor under like circumstances.
Ethics centers on competence (only practice within your expertise), independence/objectivity, confidentiality, and honesty in certifications.
Negligence requires duty, breach (falling below the standard of care), causation, and damages; standards of practice help define the duty.
A clear written contract defining scope, deliverables, and certifications is the best protection against disputes and liability.

content.html:
The surveyor as a professional

Licensure exists to protect the public: a surveyor's work fixes property rights and public safety, so the law imposes duties beyond ordinary contract. The exam tests your grasp of the standard of care, the code of ethics, and the basics of liability and business practice. The recurring theme is that the surveyor owes duties to the client, to third parties who foreseeably rely on the work, and to the public—and that those duties are measured against what a competent peer would do.

Standard of care and ethics

The standard of care is the degree of skill and diligence a reasonably prudent surveyor would exercise under the same circumstances—not perfection, but competence consistent with prevailing practice and the applicable standards (in Texas, 22 TAC Ch. 138 standards of practice). Core ethical duties flow from this:

  • Competence: practice only within your area of expertise; decline or associate help otherwise.
  • Independence / objectivity: serve as a faithful agent but never falsify or shade results to please a client.
  • Confidentiality: protect client information.
  • Honesty in certifications: sign and seal only work you performed or supervised, and certify only what you actually determined.

A certification is a professional representation—sealing a survey you did not control, or certifying beyond what you measured, is both an ethics violation and a liability exposure.

content.text: Texas differs: the binding ethical and technical standards are TBPELS rules in 22 TAC Ch. 138 (with the TSPS Manual of Practice as guidance). Cite the TAC standards on Texas-specific questions.

content.html:
Liability and business practice

Most professional claims sound in negligence or breach of contract. Negligence has four elements: a duty, a breach (falling below the standard of care), causation, and damages. Standards of practice matter because they help a court define the duty—violating a published standard is strong evidence of breach. Protect yourself with sound business practice: a written contract that defines scope, deliverables, and limitations; clear, accurate certifications matched to the work; adequate records and field-note retention; and professional liability (E&O) insurance. A precise scope statement is your best defense—most disputes arise from unmet expectations the contract never addressed.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The surveyor's paramount duty is to protect the public; the legal measure is the standard of care of a reasonably prudent surveyor.
Ethics: competence within expertise, independence, confidentiality, and honest certifications—seal only work you controlled.
Negligence = duty + breach + causation + damages; published standards help define the duty.
A written contract defining scope and deliverables, plus good records, is the strongest liability protection.

flashcards.definition: The degree of skill and diligence a reasonably prudent surveyor would exercise under similar circumstances; the yardstick for negligence claims.
Duty, breach (falling below the standard of care), causation, and damages—all four must be present for a negligence claim to succeed.
A surveyor may sign and seal only work performed under their responsible supervision and may certify only facts actually determined—not guarantees or facts beyond the survey.

lessons.title: Texas Law and Regulations Review
learning_objectives: Identify TBPELS, Occupations Code Ch. 1071, and 22 TAC Ch. 138 as the framework governing Texas surveyors.
Distinguish the RPLS, SIT, and LSLS credentials and their roles.
Apply key Texas boundary doctrines: priority of calls (Stafford), riparian/accretion rules, and the recording act.
Recall Texas-specific units, grant systems, and the GLO's role in original surveys.
key_takeaways: Texas surveyors are licensed under Occupations Code Ch. 1071 by TBPELS, with standards of practice in 22 TAC Ch. 138.
Credentials ladder: SIT (intern) → RPLS (boundary practice) → LSLS (original state-land boundaries, filing in the GLO).
Texas priority of calls traces to Stafford v. King; titles trace to sovereign grants/patents, not the PLSS.
Riparian boundaries shift with slow accretion/erosion but not with sudden avulsion; navigable streams follow the gradient boundary.

content.html:
The Texas regulatory framework in one view

This lesson consolidates the Texas-specific law an SIT must carry into the exam. Three pillars define the regulatory side: the agency (TBPELS, the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors), the statute (Occupations Code Ch. 1071, the Professional Land Surveying Practices Act), and the rules (Texas Administrative Code, Title 22, Ch. 138, standards of practice and conduct). The statute creates the license and the discipline power; the rules implement it. Memorize these three anchors—most 'which body governs?' questions resolve to them.

rows: Work under an RPLS while gaining experience; passed the FS exam.
Independent boundary surveying; seal surveys and descriptions.
Survey original state-land grant boundaries; file field notes in the GLO.

content.html:
Texas boundary doctrines worth memorizing

On the substantive side, Texas applies several well-settled doctrines: the priority of calls (natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > bearing > distance > area) grounded in Stafford v. King; senior rights for conflicting deeds from a common grantor; and the notice recording act (Prop. Code §13.001) protecting a bona fide purchaser. For water boundaries, the line follows the gradient boundary on navigable streams; a riparian boundary moves with slow, imperceptible accretion and erosion but does not move with avulsion (a sudden, perceptible shift), where the old boundary is retained. Texas titles trace to sovereign grants and patents archived at the GLO, not to a federal PLSS—so 'townships and ranges' generally do not apply.

content.text: Texas differs (units/history): Texas uses the vara (~33⅓ inches) in old grants and the league/labor for grant areas; original boundaries trace to Spanish/Mexican/Republic grants—not PLSS sections. Watch for vara-based record distances.

content.html:
Water boundaries: accretion vs. avulsion

Riparian boundary rules are heavily tested. Where land borders water, the boundary generally moves with the water when the change is slow and imperceptible—accretion (gradual deposit, owner gains) and erosion (gradual loss, owner loses). The boundary does not move when the change is sudden and perceptible—avulsion, where the old boundary line is fixed in place even though the water has shifted. Reliction (gradual withdrawal of water exposing land) is treated like accretion. On navigable Texas streams, the legal line is the gradient boundary (a mid-bank line defined by the water's gradient), and the bed below it may belong to the State. These rules turn on the rate of change, so exam scenarios hinge on whether the shift was gradual or sudden.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Framework: TBPELS agency, Occupations Code Ch. 1071 statute, 22 TAC Ch. 138 rules.
Credentials: SIT → RPLS → LSLS (LSLS for original state-land boundaries and GLO field notes).
Priority of calls (Stafford v. King) and senior rights govern boundary conflicts; titles trace to sovereign grants.
Accretion/erosion (gradual) move the boundary; avulsion (sudden) does not; navigable streams use the gradient boundary.

flashcards.definition: SIT is the surveyor-in-training intern step; RPLS is the standard Texas license to perform independent boundary surveying; LSLS is an additional credential to survey original state-land boundaries and file field notes in the GLO.

Accretion (and erosion) is gradual, imperceptible change that moves the riparian boundary with the water; avulsion is sudden, perceptible change that does NOT move the boundary—the old line is retained.
flashcards.hint_1: Slow moves the line; sudden fixes it.

flashcards.definition: The legal boundary along a navigable Texas stream—a mid-bank line defined by the water's gradient—below which the bed may be State-owned.

lessons.title: SIT Practice Problems — Set 2
learning_objectives: Solve mixed FS/SIT computational problems across coordinate systems, geodesy, curves, and photogrammetry.
Convert between grid and ground distances using the combined scale factor.
Compute orthometric height from ellipsoidal height and geoid undulation (H = h − N).
Apply the inverse to compute distance and azimuth between coordinate pairs.
key_takeaways: Ground distance = grid distance ÷ CSF, where CSF = grid scale factor × elevation factor; ground is usually longer than grid.
Orthometric height H = h − N: subtract the (often negative) geoid undulation from the ellipsoidal height.
Inverse: distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN); azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN), normalized to 0–360°.
Always carry units and a sketch; estimate the answer's magnitude before computing to catch gross errors.

content.html:
How to attack the computational set

This set drills the high-frequency calculations on the FS/SIT exam. For each problem: sketch it, write the governing formula, list knowns with units, estimate the answer, then compute. Estimating first catches the classic blunders—dividing when you should multiply, or applying a scale factor the wrong way. The worked templates below (and the module's practice generator) cover grid/ground conversion via the combined scale factor, orthometric height, horizontal-curve geometry, GSD, the coordinate inverse, and vertical-curve elevation.

content.label: Grid↔ground via combined scale factor (k = grid scale factor, h = elevation)

content.html:
Grid vs. ground

State plane coordinates live on a grid: a conformal projection (Lambert or transverse Mercator) of a flat plane scaled to the ellipsoid. To convert a measured ground distance to grid (or back), apply the combined scale factor (CSF) = grid scale factor (k) × elevation (sea-level) factor, where the elevation factor ≈ R/(R+h). Because both factors are usually slightly less than 1, grid distances are typically shorter than ground: Dground = Dgrid / CSF and Dgrid = Dground × CSF. The most common exam mistake is applying the factor in the wrong direction—remember that going up to the ground (higher elevation, real-world distance) makes the distance longer, so you divide grid by CSF.

content.label: Orthometric height from ellipsoidal height and geoid undulation

content.html:
Orthometric height (H = h − N)

GNSS gives ellipsoidal height h (above the reference ellipsoid). What we use for grades and drainage is orthometric height H (above the geoid, i.e., 'mean sea level'). They differ by the geoid undulation N, the geoid−ellipsoid separation: H = h − N. In the conterminous U.S., N is generally negative (the geoid lies below the ellipsoid), so subtracting a negative N increases H. Watch the sign carefully—mis-handling the sign of N is the most common error. A geoid model (e.g., GEOID18) supplies N at your location.

content.text: Exam tip for the inverse: azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) with ΔE = E2−E1 and ΔN = N2−N1; add 360° if negative. Note ΔE is the FIRST argument (east over north).

content.alt: Diagram of ellipsoid, geoid, and topographic surface showing h, N, and H relationships
content.caption: Figure: Relationship of ellipsoidal height h, geoid undulation N, and orthometric height H (H = h − N).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Ground = grid ÷ CSF; grid = ground × CSF; CSF = grid scale factor × elevation factor.
H = h − N; in the U.S. N is usually negative, so H comes out larger than h.
Inverse: distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN), azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) normalized to 0–360°.
Sketch, estimate, and track units before computing to catch gross errors.

flashcards.definition: The product of the grid scale factor and the elevation (sea-level) factor used to convert between ground and grid distances: ground = grid ÷ CSF.
The separation between the geoid and the reference ellipsoid; used to convert ellipsoidal height to orthometric height via H = h − N. In the U.S. N is typically negative.
Computing the distance and azimuth between two coordinate pairs: distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN); azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN), normalized to 0–360°.

lessons.title: SIT Full-Length Practice Exam Tips
learning_objectives: Describe the FS/SIT exam format, timing, and the use of the supplied reference handbook.
Apply time-management and triage strategies during the exam.
Use unit-checking, estimation, and answer-screening to avoid common computational traps.
Build an effective final-week and exam-day preparation plan.
key_takeaways: The FS exam is computer-based, multiple-choice, with a supplied reference handbook—know the handbook's layout, not just the formulas.
Triage: answer easy questions first, flag and return to hard ones; never leave a blank (no penalty for guessing).
Guard against trap answers by checking units, estimating magnitude, and confirming the scale-factor/sign direction.
Budget roughly the same time per question; if stuck past your budget, mark and move on.

content.html:
Know the format before you study

The Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam—the gateway to becoming an SIT—is a computer-based, multiple-choice exam administered by NCEES, with a generous time allowance split across the day and a built-in break. Critically, it is open to a supplied reference handbook only: you do not bring your own materials, and the handbook contains the equations and constants you are expected to use. The single highest-leverage prep step is to study the NCEES FS Reference Handbook itself—learn where each topic, formula, and table lives so you can find them in seconds under time pressure. Knowing a formula is worthless if you waste two minutes locating it.

Time management and triage

With a fixed number of questions and a fixed clock, pace is everything. Compute a rough per-question budget (total time ÷ number of questions) and respect it. Make two passes: on the first pass, answer every question you can solve quickly and flag the rest; on the second pass, return to flagged items with the time that remains. Never leave a blank—there is no penalty for guessing, so a marked-and-moved question still gets an answer. If you are past your budget on a single problem, mark it and move on; one hard problem is not worth three easy ones you never reached.

  • Easy first, hard flagged.
  • One per budget; if stuck, guess and flag.
  • Reserve the last minutes to ensure no blanks remain.

content.text: Exam tip: distractor answers are often the result of a predictable error (wrong scale-factor direction, wrong sign on N, PI+T instead of PC+L, degrees vs. radians). Recognizing the trap tells you which answer NOT to pick.

content.html:
Avoiding computational traps

Most wrong answers on a well-written exam are deliberate traps built from common mistakes. Defend against them with three habits: check units (a feet/meters mix-up is the most common slip), estimate the magnitude before computing (is the answer plausibly that big?), and verify direction/sign on the operations that flip students—dividing vs. multiplying by a scale factor, the sign of the geoid undulation, stationing along the arc (PC + L) versus the tangent (PI + T), and degrees vs. radians in trig. When two answer choices differ by exactly a known factor, that gap usually signals a units or scale-factor trap. Reading every question twice—once for the scenario, once for exactly what is asked (e.g., 'ground' vs. 'grid', 'cut' vs. 'fill')—catches the rest.

Final-week and exam-day plan

In the last week, shift from learning new material to full-length timed practice and handbook navigation drills. Sleep and logistics matter: confirm your test center, ID, and start time; the night before, rest rather than cram. On exam day, arrive early, do an easy warm-up problem first to settle nerves, take the scheduled break, hydrate, and keep moving—momentum beats perfection. Trust your two-pass system; a calm, well-paced test-taker who never leaves a blank will outperform a faster one who panics on hard problems.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The FS exam is computer-based MC with a supplied reference handbook—master the handbook's layout.
Use a per-question time budget and a two-pass (easy-first, flag-the-rest) strategy; never leave a blank.
Beat trap answers with unit checks, magnitude estimates, and sign/direction verification.
In the final week, do timed full-length practice and handbook drills; rest and arrive early on exam day.

flashcards.definition: The only reference allowed during the FS exam; it supplies the equations, constants, and tables. Effective prep means learning where each topic lives in the handbook.
Answer all quick questions on the first pass and flag harder ones, then return to flagged items on a second pass—ensuring no blanks and best use of time.
An incorrect option engineered from a common error—wrong scale-factor direction, wrong sign, degrees/radians mix-up—designed to catch a careless solver.

quiz_questions.question_text: When the elements of a deed conflict, which element generally controls under the Texas priority of calls?
quiz_questions.explanation: The priority of calls (Stafford v. King) ranks natural monuments highest, then artificial monuments, adjoinder, course, distance, and finally area/quantity. Monuments control measurements.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two parcels were carved from a common grantor and their record dimensions overlap. Under senior rights, who is entitled to the full record dimensions?
quiz_questions.explanation: Senior rights give the earliest conveyance its full record; the junior parcel takes only the remainder, absorbing any shortage in the overlap area.

quiz_questions.question_text: A found original monument's position can be recovered from accessories and reliable testimony even though the physical mark is gone. This corner is best described as:
quiz_questions.explanation: An obliterated corner has no mark but can be recovered from acceptable evidence. Only a lost corner—recoverable from no evidence—is restored by proportionate measurement.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: A retracing surveyor should compute a mathematically 'better' line rather than follow the original surveyor's erroneous footsteps.
quiz_questions.explanation: Retracement honors the original footsteps and the boundary the parties accepted, even if the original work contained errors. The goal is to locate the existing boundary, not create a new one.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a metes-and-bounds call that recites both a found monument and a bearing/distance that disagree, which controls?
quiz_questions.explanation: Specific calls (monuments) control general calls (bearing/distance). A found, identified monument fixes the location over a mere measurement.

quiz_questions.question_text: A description is clear on its face but, when applied to the ground, two different monuments fit one call. This is an example of a:
quiz_questions.explanation: A latent ambiguity is not apparent on the page but emerges when applying the words to the ground; courts admit extrinsic evidence to resolve it. A patent ambiguity is apparent on the face.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of legal description identifies a parcel by reference to a recorded subdivision map?
quiz_questions.explanation: A lot-and-block description incorporates a recorded plat by reference (e.g., Lot 7, Block C). The plat supplies dimensions and monuments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where are the original field notes and patents for a Texas land grant archived?
quiz_questions.explanation: The GLO is the archive of original Texas land-grant records—patents, field notes, and sketches—organized by survey and abstract. Subsequent deeds are recorded at the county clerk.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas is which type of recording-act jurisdiction?
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Prop. Code §13.001, Texas is a notice state: an unrecorded conveyance is void as to a subsequent bona fide purchaser for value without notice.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Most original Texas land titles trace to the federal Public Land Survey System (PLSS) of townships and ranges.
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas retained its public domain and uses sovereign grants/patents archived at the GLO, organized by surveys and abstracts—not the federal PLSS.

quiz_questions.question_text: A simple circular curve has R = 500 ft and I = 30°. What is the tangent distance T?
On a simple curve, the station of the PT is computed as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Stationing runs along the arc, so PT = PC + L. Using PI + T overstates the alignment because the arc is shorter than going out to the PI and back.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which field stake marks the catch point where a designed cut or fill slope intersects existing ground?
quiz_questions.explanation: A slope stake locates the catch point of the design slope on existing ground, set by trial. Grade and offset stakes carry cut/fill to design grade at or beside the centerline.

quiz_questions.question_text: A UAS camera has a pixel pitch of 0.0035 mm and a 35 mm focal length, flying at 120 m AGL. What is the GSD (cm/pixel)?
Which statement about contour lines is correct?
options: Closely spaced contours indicate steep terrain
Contours may cross one another on steep slopes
A single contour can represent two different elevations
Contours always point downhill when crossing a stream
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Closely spaced contours indicate steep terrain
quiz_questions.explanation: Spacing encodes slope—close contours are steep. Contours never cross or branch, each has a single elevation, and they V upstream (toward higher ground) when crossing a drainage.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a Digital Terrain Model (DTM/DEM) represent, as opposed to a DSM?
options: The surface including vegetation and buildings
A 2D planimetric map with no elevations
quiz_questions.explanation: A DTM/DEM is bare earth; a DSM includes vegetation and structures on top of the ground. Breaklines force the surface to honor grade breaks.

quiz_questions.question_text: The legal yardstick used to judge whether a surveyor was negligent is the:
options: Standard of care of a reasonably prudent surveyor
Perfection standard with zero tolerance for error
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Standard of care of a reasonably prudent surveyor
quiz_questions.explanation: Negligence is measured against the skill and diligence a reasonably prudent surveyor would exercise under similar circumstances—not perfection or client satisfaction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is NOT one of the four elements required to prove negligence?
quiz_questions.explanation: Negligence requires duty, breach, causation, and damages. Intent to harm is not an element—negligence is about failing the standard of care, not intentional wrongdoing.

quiz_questions.question_text: A river bordering a tract shifts suddenly during a flood, cutting a new channel overnight. Under boundary law, the riparian boundary:
options: Does not move (avulsion retains the old line)
Moves to the midpoint of the old and new channels
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Does not move (avulsion retains the old line)
quiz_questions.explanation: A sudden, perceptible change is avulsion, which does NOT move the boundary—the old line is retained. Only slow, imperceptible accretion/erosion moves the boundary with the water.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which credential is required to survey original state-land grant boundaries and file field notes in the GLO?
quiz_questions.explanation: The LSLS is the additional credential needed to survey original state-land boundaries and file field notes with the GLO; an RPLS alone is not sufficient for original state-land work.

quiz_questions.question_text: A grid distance is 2000.00 ft and the combined scale factor is 0.9998. What is the corresponding ground distance (ft)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Ground = grid ÷ CSF = 2000.00 / 0.9998 = 2000.40 ft. Ground is longer than grid because CSF < 1.

quiz_questions.question_text: A GNSS observation gives an ellipsoidal height h = 312.40 m at a point where the geoid undulation N = −25.60 m. What is the orthometric height H (m)?
quiz_questions.explanation: H = h − N = 312.40 − (−25.60) = 338.00 m. Subtracting a negative N increases H.

quiz_questions.question_text: Given point A (N 5000.00, E 4000.00) and point B (N 5300.00, E 4400.00), what is the inverse distance A→B (ft)?
quiz_questions.explanation: distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN) = sqrt(400² + 300²) = sqrt(250000) = 500.00 ft (a 3-4-5 triangle).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which exam strategy best uses the supplied reference handbook on the FS exam?
options: Learn where each formula and table lives so you can find it quickly
Memorize the entire handbook word for word
Bring your own annotated copy to the test center
Avoid the handbook and rely on memory only
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Learn where each formula and table lives so you can find it quickly
quiz_questions.explanation: The FS exam allows only the supplied handbook. The highest-leverage prep is mastering its layout so you can locate equations and constants in seconds under time pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Because there is no penalty for guessing on the FS exam, you should never leave a question blank.
quiz_questions.explanation: There is no penalty for guessing, so every question should receive an answer. The two-pass strategy ensures flagged questions still get a marked answer before time expires.

problem_templates.name: Grid distance to ground distance via combined scale factor
problem_templates.question_template: A line measures {{grid:f2}} ft on the state plane grid. The grid scale factor is {{k:f6}} and the elevation (sea-level) factor is {{ef:f6}}. Using the combined scale factor, what is the ground distance (ft)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Ground distance = grid ÷ CSF, where CSF = {{k:f6}} × {{ef:f6}} = {{csf:f6}}. So {{grid:f2}} ÷ {{csf:f6}} = {{_answer}} ft. Because CSF < 1, the ground distance is slightly longer than the grid distance.

images_needed.caption: Grid line vs. ground line with combined scale factor

problem_templates.name: Orthometric height from ellipsoidal height and geoid undulation
problem_templates.question_template: A GNSS observation gives an ellipsoidal height of {{h:f2}} m. The geoid model reports a geoid undulation of {{N:f2}} m at the point. What is the orthometric height H (m)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Orthometric height H = h − N = {{h:f2}} − ({{N:f2}}) = {{_answer}} m. Because N is negative here, subtracting it increases H.

images_needed.caption: Ellipsoid, geoid, and topo surface showing h, N, H

problem_templates.name: Horizontal circular curve — tangent distance
problem_templates.question_template: A simple circular curve has a radius of {{R}} ft and an intersection angle I = {{I:f2}}°. What is the tangent distance T (ft)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: T = R·tan(I/2) = {{R}} × tan({{I:f2}}°/2) = {{_answer}} ft. The PC lies T feet back from the PI along the back tangent.

images_needed.caption: Simple curve with PI, PC, PT, T, and intersection angle I

problem_templates.name: Horizontal circular curve — arc length
problem_templates.question_template: A simple circular curve has a radius of {{R}} ft and an intersection angle I = {{I:f2}}°. What is the arc length L (ft)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Arc length L = R·I in radians = {{R}} × toRad({{I:f2}}°) = {{_answer}} ft. Station PT = PC + L (along the arc, not PI + T).

images_needed.caption: Curve arc length spanning the intersection angle

problem_templates.name: Ground Sample Distance (GSD) from flight parameters
problem_templates.question_template: A UAS camera has a pixel pitch of {{p:f4}} mm and a focal length of {{f:f1}} mm, flying at {{H}} m above ground level. What is the Ground Sample Distance (cm/pixel)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: GSD = (p × H) / f = ({{p:f4}} mm × {{Hmm}} mm) ÷ {{f:f1}} mm = {{gsdmm:f2}} mm = {{_answer}} cm/pixel. Lower flight height gives a smaller (finer) GSD.

images_needed.caption: Similar-triangle geometry of pixel pitch, focal length, flight height, and ground footprint

problem_templates.name: Inverse distance between two coordinate pairs
problem_templates.question_template: Point A is at (N {{n1:f2}}, E {{e1:f2}}) and point B is at (N {{n2:f2}}, E {{e2:f2}}). What is the horizontal distance from A to B (ft)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: The inverse distance = hypot(ΔE, ΔN) = sqrt({{de:f2}}² + {{dn:f2}}²) = {{_answer}} ft. Azimuth would be atan2(ΔE, ΔN), normalized to 0–360°.

images_needed.caption: Right triangle of ΔN, ΔE, and the inverse distance between two points

problem_templates.name: Vertical curve elevation at a station past the BVC
problem_templates.question_template: An equal-tangent vertical curve of length {{L}} stations joins an incoming grade g1 = {{g1:f2}}% to an outgoing grade g2 = {{g2:f2}}%. The BVC elevation is {{ebvc:f2}} ft. What is the curve elevation {{x:f2}} stations past the BVC (ft)?
problem_templates.answer_formula: ebvc + g1 * x + (g2 - g1) / (2 * L) * x * x
problem_templates.explanation_template: On an equal-tangent vertical curve, elevation = Ebvc + g1·x + (A/2L)·x² with A = g2 − g1 = {{A:f2}}%, x and L in stations. Result = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Equal-tangent parabolic vertical curve with BVC, grades g1/g2, and the offset at station x
